aligned_pages.ppt_text:
Training Error and Generalization Error
• Training error (bias): model error on the training data
• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
• Example: practice a future exam with past exams
• Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn’t guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
• Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers

bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduces this topic by motivating it with a concrete observation: a 6-layer model may actually perform worse than a 2-layer model, which seems counterintuitive given that deeper networks are supposed to be more powerful. This paradox sets the stage for the entire discussion on practical aspects of training neural networks. The teacher signals that simply adding more layers is not enough — there are real-world training challenges that must be understood and addressed. This section is significant because it bridges theory (deeper = more expressive) with practice (deeper ≠ always better without proper techniques).

generated_pages.ppt_text: Predict Who Will Repay TheirLoans •A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans •You are given complete files on 100 •5 defaulted within 3 years Image creditdebt.org

aligned_segments.text: That is, I may want to predict who will repay their loan and who will default.
So in such a scenario, a lender helps you to investigate.
This problem.
And you are given the files of one hundred applicants.
Among these five, one hundred applicants, there are five of them defaulted within three years.

bullets.ai_comment: The overarching goal of this slide is to predict which loan applicants will repay their loans and which will default. The teacher frames this as a practical, real-world classification problem where the outcome has direct financial consequences. This is a classic example of supervised binary classification in machine learning. Understanding who will default helps lenders minimize financial risk and make better lending decisions.

bullets.ppt_text: A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher sets up a scenario where a lender — a bank or financial institution — brings you in as a data scientist to solve a real business problem. Your job is to build a model that can distinguish reliable borrowers from risky ones. This mirrors how machine learning is applied in the fintech and banking industry today. The practical framing emphasizes that ML models serve concrete organizational needs, not just academic curiosity.

bullets.ppt_text: You are given complete files on 100
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher specifies that you have access to complete application files for 100 loan applicants, which serves as your training dataset. These files likely contain features such as income, credit history, employment status, and other financial indicators. Having 'complete' files means there is no missing data in this simplified example, which is important for building a clean initial model. In practice, datasets are rarely this clean, but this setup helps illustrate the core concept without additional complexity.

Out of the 100 applicants, exactly 5 defaulted on their loans within a 3-year period, meaning 95 successfully repaid. The teacher highlights this to draw attention to a critical challenge in machine learning known as class imbalance — the two outcome classes (default vs. repay) are very unevenly distributed (5% vs. 95%). This imbalance matters enormously because a naive model could achieve 95% accuracy simply by predicting 'repay' for everyone, yet still be completely useless at catching defaulters. Addressing class imbalance is a key real-world concern in credit risk modeling.

A Surprising Finding
• All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
• Your model may find this strong signal as well

bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduces a counterintuitive scenario to illustrate a fundamental problem in machine learning model building. The setup involves predicting loan default behavior, where the data leads to a seemingly absurd but statistically tempting conclusion. This section serves as a cautionary tale about blindly trusting patterns a model discovers in data. Understanding this concept is critical before building any predictive model in practice.

bullets.ppt_text: • All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher describes a scenario where a dataset contains photos of loan applicants, and it turns out that every single one of the five people who defaulted happened to be wearing a blue shirt during their interview. This is purely a coincidence arising from the tiny sample size — with only five defaulters, any random attribute they share (shirt color, hair length, etc.) could appear as a pattern. The teacher emphasizes that you cannot conclude 'only defaulters wear blue shirts' just because all defaulters in your sample did, since non-defaulters may have also worn blue shirts. This example highlights how small datasets make spurious correlations dangerously easy to find.

bullets.ppt_text: • Your model may find this strong signal as well
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher warns that a machine learning model, having no human intuition or common sense, will treat 'wearing a blue shirt' as a genuinely strong predictive feature for default if the pattern exists in the training data. This is a classic example of overfitting — the model latches onto noise or coincidence rather than a true causal relationship. The teacher further applies human logic: we inherently know shirt color has no causal link to financial default behavior, yet the model cannot make this judgment on its own. This illustrates why domain knowledge and feature validation are just as important as model performance metrics when building reliable predictive systems.

Training Error and Generalization Error

• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data

aligned_segments.text: And this introduce our classic discussion of training error and generalization error.
Training error means, the error on the training data set and generalization error refers to your error on a new data.
And we always say that the performance on the training data does not represents the performance on a new data set.
This is very similar to we make practices for a.

bullets.ai_comment: This slide introduces one of the most fundamental concepts in machine learning: the distinction between how well a model performs on data it has already seen versus data it has never seen. The teacher frames this as a "classic discussion," signaling its central importance in the field. Understanding this distinction is the foundation for diagnosing underfitting and overfitting in any ML pipeline.

bullets.ppt_text: • Training error (bias): model error on the training data
bullets.ai_comment: Training error is the error a model makes when evaluated on the exact data it was trained on. The teacher explains it simply as "the error on the training data set." Because the model has already "seen" this data during learning, training error tends to be optimistically low and is not a reliable indicator of real-world performance. It is sometimes associated with bias — a model with very high training error likely underfits the data.

bullets.ppt_text: • Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
bullets.ai_comment: Generalization error measures how well the model performs on data it has never encountered before, which is the true goal of machine learning. The teacher defines it as "your error on a new data," emphasizing the word 'new' to contrast it with training data. A large gap between training error and generalization error indicates overfitting — the model has memorized the training set rather than learning underlying patterns. Minimizing generalization error is ultimately what makes a model useful in production.

bullets.ppt_text: • Example: practice a future exam with past exams
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher uses the analogy of a student preparing for an upcoming exam by practicing with past exams, which maps directly onto the ML setting: past exams are the training data, and the future exam is the unseen test data. This analogy was introduced starting around the 04:55 mark, though the transcript cuts off mid-sentence. It is a highly intuitive framing because every student has experienced the difference between feeling confident after practice and then facing unexpected questions on the real exam.

bullets.ppt_text: • Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn't guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
bullets.ai_comment: This bullet is the core takeaway of the analogy: low training error does not imply low generalization error. The teacher explicitly states "the performance on the training data does not represent the performance on a new data set," directly mirroring this point. In ML, a model can achieve near-zero training error yet fail badly on new inputs if it has simply memorized the training examples. This is why evaluating models on held-out test sets is an essential practice.

bullets.ppt_text: • Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers
